beats.headline: The malware was shipped in, signed and trusted.
beats.body: Attackers hid a backdoor inside a routine software update from a trusted vendor; roughly 18,000 organizations installed it themselves. They didn't break in — they were built in.
say: Every gap, every shortcut, every forgotten door — I will find it. Or I'll have you build it for me.
Not tonight. We harden the forge itself — how systems are built, configured, and supplied.

beats.title: THE FORGE
say: Why break in, when I can be built in? A flaw here, a tampered part there...
Then we harden the forge itself, where systems are made and supplied.

beats.title: THE FORGE
beats.subtitle: Acquisition · Configuration · Maintenance · Supply Chain
say: Layer five is how you build, buy, configure, maintain, and source.
Four families work the forge: System Acquisition, Configuration Management, Maintenance, and Supply Chain Risk Management.

beats.title: THE FORGE
say: SA, CM, MA, and SR. The quartermaster, the blueprint-master, the smith, and the caravan-master.

beats.protects: Buys and builds only what can be trusted.
beats.reality: Building security into the development life cycle and into systems and services you acquire.
say: The Quartermaster, System and Services Acquisition.
It buys and builds only what can be trusted.
In reality, that's building security into the development life cycle, and into the systems and services you acquire.

beats.title: Security and Privacy Engineering Principles
beats.plain: Apply security and privacy engineering principles when you design and build.
beats.why: Security bolted on later is weak; SA-8 bakes it in from the first sketch.
say: SA-8 builds security in by design, not as an afterthought.

beats.title: Unsupported System Components
beats.plain: Replace unsupported components — software past its end of life.
beats.why: Unsupported software gets no patches; it's a permanently open window.
say: What about software no one updates anymore?
SA-22 says replace it, or arrange alternate support. Unsupported is risk you must manage.

beats.quote: Apply the following systems security and privacy engineering principles in the specification, design, development, implementation, and modification of the system and system components:
beats.cite: NIST SP 800-53r5  ·  SA-8 Security and Privacy Engineering Principles
say: Apply the following systems security and privacy engineering principles in the specification, design, development, implementation, and modification of the system and system components:

beats.title: System and Services Acquisition
bullets: SA-3 System Development Life Cycle — build security into the life cycle
SA-4 Acquisition Process — put security in acquisition contracts
SA-8 Security and Privacy Engineering Principles — engineer with security principles
SA-22 Unsupported System Components — replace unsupported components
beats.mnemonic: “Build it secure, or don't buy it.”
say: Trust is forged in, never sprinkled on.
The Quartermaster's oath. Built in, never sprinkled on.

beats.protects: Keeps the master plans and forbids unsanctioned change.
beats.reality: Baseline configurations and change control so systems stay in a known, secure state.
say: The Master of Blueprints, Configuration Management.
One quiet, undocumented change... and I have a door no one remembers.
In reality, that's keeping a known, approved configuration and controlling every change, so systems stay in a trusted state.

beats.plain: Heads up: 'baseline' means two different things. A CONTROL baseline (from 800-53B) is your starting set of controls. A CONFIGURATION baseline (CM-2) is the known-good snapshot of a system's settings, versions, and components.
say: We already met 'baseline' back in Episode 00.
Different baseline. That was the control baseline. CM-2 is the configuration baseline — drift from it, and you have a question to answer.

beats.title: Baseline Configuration
beats.plain: Keep a known, documented baseline configuration of the system.
beats.why: If you don't know the 'normal,' you can't spot the tampering.
say: CM-2 holds the baseline, the master blueprint of the system.

beats.title: Least Functionality
beats.plain: Turn off what you don't need — least functionality.
beats.why: Every extra service and port is another door; CM-7 closes the unused ones.
say: CM-7 is least functionality. Computers talk on numbered channels — ports — and run background programs — services.
And every one you don't need is another door.
Exactly. CM-7 closes the ports and services you never use.

beats.quote: Develop, document, and maintain under configuration control, a current baseline configuration of the system;
say: Develop, document, and maintain under configuration control, a current baseline configuration of the system;

beats.title: Configuration Management
bullets: CM-2 Baseline Configuration — keep a known baseline configuration
CM-3 Configuration Change Control — control every change
CM-7 Least Functionality — least functionality — disable the unneeded
CM-8 System Component Inventory — inventory your components
say: A known system is a defensible system.

beats.oath: A known system is a defended system.
say: The Blueprint-Master's oath. A known system is a defended system.

beats.protects: Tends the machinery, and watches who holds the tools.
beats.reality: Controlled system maintenance, including safeguards for remote and third-party maintenance.
say: It tends the machinery, and watches who holds the tools.
In reality, that's controlled system maintenance — including safeguards for remote and third-party work.

beats.title: Controlled Maintenance
beats.plain: Control and log maintenance — who, what, when, and approved.
beats.why: Maintenance touches the guts of a system; uncontrolled, it's a perfect cover.
say: MA-2 controls maintenance, scheduled, approved, and recorded.

beats.title: Nonlocal Maintenance
beats.why: A remote maintenance link is a powerful door; MA-4 keeps it locked and watched.
say: Your remote support line was going to be my way in.
MA-4 saw to that. Remote maintenance, tightly controlled.

beats.quote: Schedule, document, and review records of maintenance, repair, and replacement on system components in accordance with manufacturer or vendor specifications and/or organizational requirements;
say: Schedule, document, and review records of maintenance, repair, and replacement on system components in accordance with manufacturer or vendor specifications and/or organizational requirements;

beats.title: Maintenance
bullets: MA-2 Controlled Maintenance — control and record maintenance
MA-4 Nonlocal Maintenance — secure remote maintenance
MA-5 Maintenance Personnel — vet maintenance personnel
say: Maintenance is access. Treat it like access.

beats.oath: Every tool is a key, and I guard the keys.
say: The Smith's oath. Every tool is a key, and I guard the keys.

beats.protects: Guards the roads your supplies travel, all the way to the source.
beats.reality: Managing risk from suppliers, components, and the supply chain: authenticity and provenance.
say: The Caravan-Master, Supply Chain Risk Management, new in Revision 5.
It guards the roads your supplies travel, all the way to the source.
In reality, that's managing risk from suppliers and components — the SolarWinds-style attack we opened with, where a trusted update carried a hidden backdoor.

beats.title: Component Authenticity
beats.why: A tampered part can arrive pre-compromised; SR-11 checks what you're given is real.
say: How do we trust the parts themselves?
SR-11, component authenticity, hunts counterfeits and tampering.

beats.quote: Develop and implement anti-counterfeit policy and procedures that include the means to detect and prevent counterfeit components from entering the system;
say: Develop and implement anti-counterfeit policy and procedures that include the means to detect and prevent counterfeit components from entering the system;

beats.title: Supply Chain Risk Management
bullets: SR-3 Supply Chain Controls and Processes — supply chain controls and processes
SR-5 Acquisition Strategies, Tools, and Methods — strategies for acquiring components
SR-8 Notification Agreements — notification agreements with suppliers
SR-11 Component Authenticity — detect and prevent counterfeits
beats.mnemonic: “Trust the road, all the way to the source.”
say: Your security is only as strong as your weakest supplier.

beats.oath: I trust no part I cannot trace.
say: The Caravan-Master's oath. I trust no part I cannot trace.

beats.title: The Forge in the RMF
bullets: CM keeps IMPLEMENTATION honest and supports MONITORING
SA and SR push security upstream — into building and buying
In practice: secure SDLC, locked-down configs, vetted suppliers
say: The best breach is the one designed out before it's built.

beats.q: A counterfeit network card arrives pre-tampered. Which control should catch it?
say: Scenario: a tampered part in the box. Whose job is that? Pause, and answer.

beats.q: Disabling unused ports and services is which control?
say: And shutting the doors you never use?

beats.title: Episode 05 — the Forge
lines: SA builds and buys security in (SA-3, SA-8, SA-22).
CM keeps a known config and controls every change (CM-2, CM-7, CM-8).
MA guards maintenance; SR guards the supply chain (MA-2, MA-4; SR-3, SR-11).
beats.mnemonic: The best breach is the one designed out before it's built.
say: Notebook: secure how you build, configure, maintain, and source — the attacker counts on you skipping one.
The forge is hardened, from source to system.
Then I'll take your secrets in transit, or your backups.
Then we ride for the Vaults and the Lifelines.
